title: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman offers four immediate abolitionist responses to the Supreme Court's Dobbs decision, arguing that while Roe's reversal is welcome, the ruling itself was wrongly decided because it treats the Constitution as neutral on abortion rather than recognizing preborn children as persons entitled to equal protection. He contends the abolitionist movement, not pro-life incrementalism, is what finally pressured the Court to overturn Roe.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue the Dobbs decision was wrongly decided despite overturning Roe?
questions.answer: Because Dobbs treats the Constitution as neutral on abortion, which Silberman argues requires accepting that preborn humans are not 'persons' protected by the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. Affirming the human non-person concept abdicates the Court's duty to provide equal protection.
questions.quote: Because the fifth and 14th amendments protect the rights to life and equal protection of all “persons,” the only way to justify the idea of Constitutional neutrality on abortion is to argue that preborn human beings are not “people” for purposes of those amendments; a preposterous assertion.

questions.question: What is the danger of accepting the 'human non-person' concept?
questions.answer: Once any class of living humans can be legally classified as non-persons, no one's rights are secure. Silberman cites the historical treatment of preborn, Jewish, and black people as proof that this concept has produced atrocity in the recent Western past.
questions.quote: If we concede the validity of the human non-person concept, then no one’s God-given, Constitutionally protected rights are safe. If the majority decides you’re a human non-person, all legal protections no longer apply to you.

questions.question: Did the Supreme Court actually need to grant permission before states could abolish abortion?
questions.answer: No. Silberman insists the states never required the Court's permission to obey Christ or uphold constitutional rights to life and equal protection, and that abolition bills could and should have been advanced regardless of Roe.
questions.quote: Of course, we [never needed the court’s permission](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html) to obey Christ or uphold the Constitutional rights to life and equal protection, but we now have it.

questions.question: Why does Silberman warn that the political fight is far from over after Dobbs?
questions.answer: Because abortion remains legal in some form in every state since none have made preborn children equal under law, and pro-life dogma against penalizing mothers leaves a self-induced abortion loophole everywhere.
questions.quote: Though abortion regulations just became much more restrictive in about half the states, it is still legal to murder a preborn children in one form or another in every state because not a single state has made preborn children equal under law. Many loopholes for abortion remain, including a loop hole of self-induced abortion which is available in every state because of the pro-life dogma that mothers should never face legal penalties for murdering their children.

questions.question: What positive shift does the article see in the post-Dobbs political landscape?
questions.answer: Legislators can no longer hide behind the Supreme Court and claim their hands are tied. The debate now turns squarely on equal protection: whether preborn children should receive the same protections as everyone else, and whether those who kill them should face appropriate penalties.
questions.quote: Now, the dispute will focus singularly on the question of equal protection: should preborn children receive the same protections and justice that all of us get? Should those who intentionally kill preborn children — including the mother — be liable to face penalties, as appropriate given the facts of the case, for doing so?

questions.question: Why does Silberman dispute incrementalist claims of credit for the Dobbs ruling?
questions.answer: He argues Dobbs was the first abortion case in which abolitionists submitted an amicus brief warning the Court that states would abolish abortion regardless, the first since the SBC affirmed an abolition resolution, and the first since Roe-defying bills were filed in over a dozen states. The pressure that produced Roe's reversal came from abolitionist demand, not from decades of incrementalist begging.
questions.quote: *Dobbs* — the case in which Roe was finally overturned — is the first abortion-related case in which an [abolitionist *amicus brief*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CstBZF-VDBE) was submitted communicating to the court that they must abolish abortion or else the states will.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the difference between the pro-life and abolitionist approaches to the Court?
questions.answer: Pro-lifers spent 49 years requesting the Court's permission, while abolitionists issued a demand. Quoting Frederick Douglass, he argues power only yields to demands, not to begging.
questions.quote: As Frederick Douglass famously said, “Power concedes nothing without a demand.” The Pro-Life Movement begged for the court’s permission for 49 years. The Abolitionist Movement made a demand: abolish abortion or else we will.

questions.question: What does the article say is required if Roe-style federal action is reimposed?
questions.answer: States must learn from past compliance, refuse to obey unconstitutional federal mandates, and proceed to abolish abortion regardless. Repenting of the idolatry of obeying man over God remains essential.
questions.quote: If that happens, we must be prepared to learn from the mistakes of the past, ignore evil and unconstitutional diktats, and abolish abortion.

questions.question: What is Silberman's theological framing for why preborn children must be granted equal protection?
questions.answer: Because all humans are created in the image of God, they are persons whose lives must be protected equally under law. Continuing to deny this is the moral and political battle that remains.
questions.quote: All humans are created in the image of God and are persons who must be provided the equal protection of the law. The battle continues until our laws and our society reflect these principles.

related.title: Analyzing Alito's Leaked Draft: How Roe Died and Where We Go from Here
related.reason: Direct precursor analysis of the same Dobbs majority opinion before its official release.

related.title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
related.reason: Earlier abolitionist analysis of the Dobbs oral arguments leading up to this decision reaction.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: Documents the abolitionist amicus brief Silberman cites as a key pressure point on the Court.

related.title: June v. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Earlier abolitionist critique of relying on the Supreme Court that frames the ignore-Roe argument expanded here.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Develops the anti-incrementalist case that underpins point 4 about why Dobbs is not an incrementalist victory.

related.title: Abolitionists Pass Resolution, Make Huge Strides at SBC Convention
related.reason: Covers the SBC abolition resolution Silberman names as one of the political shifts that pressured the Court.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the Villains of American History?
related.reason: Defends the nullification/interposition strategy that the article links to and prescribes if federal action reimposes Roe.
